summary: This PR adds support for generator-based WSGI apps by introducing a `_get_non_empty_chunk` helper that drives the generator far enough to trigger `start_response` before consuming the response. The logic is mostly sound for the common case, but there are several correctness concerns around resource leaks and fragile invariants, as well as style and test-coverage gaps worth addressing.

findings.title: start_response not guaranteed to have been called after _get_non_empty_chunk
findings.detail: When a WSGI generator app yields nothing at all, or calls `start_response` lazily (after the first yield, which is spec-permitted), `_get_non_empty_chunk` may exhaust the iterator without `start_response` ever having been called. The subsequent assertions on `seen_status` / `seen_response_headers` (lines 105-106) would then fire. For the common case—`start_response` called before the first yield—this works, but the invariant is not enforced and the failure mode is a runtime assertion error rather than a meaningful exception. The code should explicitly check that `start_response` was called and raise an informative error if not.

findings.title: Resource leak: iterator is not closed when all chunks are empty
findings.detail: PEP 3333 requires that if the iterable returned by a WSGI app has a `close()` method, the server must call it after iteration is complete. In `_get_non_empty_chunk`, `body = iter(body)` (line 13) wraps the original iterable. When the loop finds no non-empty chunk and returns `[]`, the wrapped iterator is silently abandoned—its `close()` is never called, so generator `finally` blocks and `GeneratorExit` handlers in the WSGI app will never run. The non-empty path is safe because the caller holds the chained iterator and can call `close()` on it, but the all-empty path must explicitly call `body.close()` (if available) before returning `[]`.

findings.title: Misleading function name and uninformative docstring on _get_non_empty_chunk
findings.detail: The function does not return a single chunk—it returns the full remaining iterable starting from the first non-empty chunk. A name like `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` or `_iter_from_first_non_empty` would better communicate this. The current docstring (`"Get a non-empty chunk from body."`) simply restates the (already misleading) name without explaining *why* the helper exists (i.e., the PEP 3333 requirement that `start_response` status must not be used until the first non-empty write/yield). A brief rationale would be more consistent with the project's documentation style.

findings.title: Missing tests for list-based WSGI apps with leading empty chunks and completely empty generators
findings.detail: Two distinct edge cases of `_get_non_empty_chunk` are untested: (1) a plain list-returning WSGI app that starts with empty bytes, e.g. `return [b'', b'hello']`, which would exercise the non-generator all-empty-leading path independently; and (2) a generator (or list) that yields/returns no chunks at all (`return iter([])` or `def app(): start_response(...); return`), which is a valid WSGI shape and exercises the loop-fallthrough-to-`[]` path. Both cases are distinct from the `test_wsgi_generator_empty` case (which covers all-empty-bytes output, not zero-item output).

findings.title: application_factory breaks convention of standalone, named test helpers
findings.detail: All existing test helpers (`echo_body`, `echo_body_with_response_stream`, `raise_exc`) are concrete, named functions with obvious purposes. Introducing `application_factory` requires readers to chase an indirection for each test that uses it. Defining concrete named generator helpers (e.g., `hello_world_generator`) alongside `application_factory`—or replacing the factory entirely—would be more consistent with the established style and improve readability.

findings.title: No direct unit test for _get_non_empty_chunk with multiple non-empty chunks
findings.detail: The multi-chunk non-empty case is only exercised indirectly through integration-level tests. A targeted test (or a multi-chunk app fixture) would directly validate that the chained iterator correctly yields all remaining non-empty chunks after the first, guarding against regressions in the `itertools.chain` path.

findings.title: New tests assert both response.text and response.content redundantly
findings.detail: Existing tests (e.g., `test_wsgi`, `test_wsgi_upload`) assert only `response.text`. Asserting both `response.text` and `response.content` for the same logical value is redundant and deviates from the established pattern. Pick one assertion per value.

findings.title: Inline comment duplicates information that belongs in the helper's docstring
findings.detail: The comment on lines 101-102 explains why `_get_non_empty_chunk` is called, but this rationale is more naturally expressed in the helper's own docstring (see finding above). Having both creates mild duplication; prefer one authoritative location.

findings.title: start_response may not have been called before assertions after _get_non_empty_chunk
findings.detail: When the WSGI app is a generator function, `self.app(environ, start_response)` returns a generator object immediately—without executing any of the generator body. The actual code inside the generator (including the `start_response(...)` call) only runs when the generator is first iterated. `_get_non_empty_chunk` does drive the generator to get the first non-empty chunk (which causes `start_response` to be called), but only when the app yields something. However, consider the all-empty case: `_get_non_empty_chunk` will iterate through all empty chunks, consuming the entire generator, and return `[]`. At that point `seen_status` and `seen_response_headers` will have been set (assuming `start_response` is called before the first `yield`). But if the generator calls `start_response` after the first `yield` (which is allowed by the spec), or if the generator produces no items at all and never calls `start_response`, the asserts at lines 105-106 will fire. More critically: the current code relies on `_get_non_empty_chunk` to drive the generator far enough to call `start_response`, but this is not guaranteed—the spec allows `start_response` to be called lazily. For the common case (all-empty output, `start_response` called before first yield) this works, but it is a fragile invariant that is not enforced.

findings.title: _get_non_empty_chunk does not close the iterator on early return, potentially leaking resources
findings.detail: The WSGI spec (PEP 3333) requires that if the iterable returned by the app has a `close()` method, the server must call it when it has finished iterating. In `_get_non_empty_chunk`, the original `body` iterator is wrapped with `iter()`. When `itertools.chain([chunk], body)` is returned, the caller holds a reference to the chained iterator that includes the original body—so `close()` can propagate if the caller calls it. However, if the loop exhausts without finding a non-empty chunk (the all-empty case), `[]` is returned and the original `body` iterator's `close()` is never called. This means generator-cleanup code (e.g., `finally` blocks or `GeneratorExit` handling) in the WSGI app will not run.

findings.title: Rebinding `body` parameter shadows the original iterable, preventing close() from being called on it
findings.detail: `body = iter(body)` replaces the function parameter with a plain iterator. If the original `body` object had a `close()` method (e.g., it was a generator), that method is now only reachable through the new `body` variable. When the all-empty path returns `[]`, the wrapped iterator (`body`) is abandoned without calling `close()`. Combined with the finding above, this is a resource leak for generator-based WSGI apps that return only empty chunks.

findings.title: Function name `_get_non_empty_chunk` is slightly misleading
findings.detail: The function doesn't return a single chunk — it returns the full remaining iterable starting from the first non-empty chunk. A name like `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` or `_iter_from_first_non_empty` would better communicate what the function actually produces.

findings.title: Docstring is too generic to be useful
findings.detail: The docstring `"Get a non-empty chunk from body."` mirrors the (already slightly misleading) function name without adding any new information. The surrounding code (e.g. `WSGIDispatch`'s class docstring) favours more explanatory docstrings. A brief note about why this is needed (e.g. referencing the PEP 3333 requirement that `start_response` status must not be used until the first non-empty write/yield) would be consistent with the project's style.

findings.title: Inline comment partially duplicates the module-level docstring on the helper
findings.detail: The comment on line 101-102 (`# This is needed because the status returned by start_response / # shouldn't be used until the first non-empty chunk has been served.`) is reasonable, but it repeats the purpose already described (or that should be described) in the helper's own docstring. Keeping the explanation only in the docstring, or only here, would avoid the duplication.

findings.title: `application_factory` replaces the concrete `hello_world` fixture with a generic factory, reducing readability
findings.detail: The existing test helpers (`echo_body`, `echo_body_with_response_stream`, `raise_exc`) are all standalone, named functions with clear purposes. Introducing a factory breaks that convention and makes each individual test slightly harder to follow because you have to look up what `application_factory` produces. Keeping a concrete `hello_world` function (possibly as a generator) alongside a separate generator-specific helper would be more consistent with the existing style.

findings.title: New tests assert both `response.text` and `response.content`, unlike existing tests
findings.detail: Existing test functions (e.g. `test_wsgi`, `test_wsgi_upload`) only assert `response.text`. Asserting both `response.text` and `response.content` for the same value is redundant and deviates from the pattern established in the rest of the file.

findings.title: No test for non-generator (list-based) WSGI app returning leading empty chunks
findings.detail: _get_non_empty_chunk is generic and works on any iterable, but the only tests exercising it use the generator-based application_factory. A plain list-returning WSGI app (like the original hello_world) that yields leading empty bytes is never tested through this helper. For example, an app that does `return [b'', b'hello']` would exercise the list branch of _get_non_empty_chunk independently of the generator path.

findings.title: No test for _get_non_empty_chunk with a single non-empty first chunk (no leading empties)
findings.detail: The new test_wsgi() test calls application_factory([b"Hello, World!"]) which goes through _get_non_empty_chunk but only with a single non-empty element, so the chain([chunk], body) path is taken immediately with an already-exhausted tail. The more interesting path — first chunk non-empty, remaining chunks also non-empty — is covered by test_wsgi_generator indirectly, but a direct unit test of _get_non_empty_chunk itself (or a multi-chunk all-non-empty case) is absent.

findings.title: No test covering a WSGI app whose generator raises StopIteration immediately (completely empty generator)
findings.detail: The for-loop in _get_non_empty_chunk falls through and returns [] when every chunk is empty or the iterable is exhausted. test_wsgi_generator_empty covers the 'all empty bytes' case, but a WSGI generator that yields nothing at all (i.e., an empty generator with zero iterations) is not tested. This is a distinct and valid WSGI app shape (e.g. `def app(...): start_response(...); return iter([])` or a generator that never yields).

findings.title: start_response not guaranteed to have been called after _get_non_empty_chunk
findings.detail: When a WSGI generator app yields nothing at all, or calls `start_response` lazily (after the first yield, which is spec-permitted), `_get_non_empty_chunk` may exhaust the iterator without `start_response` ever having been called.
